params.text:
What this means for your everyday work

The MCA and DoLS are not just paperwork for managers — they shape how you support people every single day. Good practice looks like this:

  • Assume capacity and offer real support to help people decide for themselves.
  • Respect unwise choices made by people who can decide — it is their right.
  • Only move to a best-interests decision when capacity for that specific decision is genuinely lacking, and always start from the person’s own wishes, feelings and values.
  • Choose the least restrictive option, and use restraint only when it is necessary and proportionate to prevent harm to the person.
  • Recognise when restrictions may amount to a deprivation of liberty, and make sure it is properly authorised.
  • Record your observations, and raise concerns with your manager or safeguarding lead if you think someone’s rights are not being protected.

When in doubt, ask. Acting under the MCA in good faith and in line with its principles protects the person — and protects you.

params.question: The Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS) have now replaced DoLS, so DoLS no longer applies.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. LPS was legislated for but has never been implemented. DoLS is still the law in force and is the framework you must apply.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Although LPS was passed in law, it has not been brought into force. DoLS remains the legal framework you must follow.

params.question: Which of these best sums up the aim of the Mental Capacity Act?

answers.text: To keep decision-making with the person wherever possible, and to protect and empower those who cannot decide for themselves.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The MCA is about empowerment and protection — supporting people to decide, and safeguarding their best interests and rights when they cannot.

answers.text: To give care staff and families the power to make decisions for anyone with a diagnosis such as dementia.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. A diagnosis never removes decision-making rights. The MCA starts from the presumption of capacity and only allows others to decide for a specific decision the person genuinely cannot make.

answers.text: To make sure services can always choose the safest, most convenient option for the people they support.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The MCA centres on the person’s own wishes and the least restrictive option — not on what is safest or most convenient for the service.

answers.text: To allow restraint to be used freely whenever staff feel it would help.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Restraint is tightly limited — it must be necessary to prevent harm to the person and proportionate to the likelihood and seriousness of that harm.
